transcripts.content:
[00:00:00]
Full name? Isabella, I-S-A-B-E-L-L-E-A. I'm 14 years old. I go to Mitchell High School and I live in Seven Hills.
What do you like doing? I like being very artsy. I'm a very artsy person. I like sport, music and I like eating a lot.
What don't you like about this area? I don't like how there's a lot of gang violence and all of that. 'cause every week there's at least something new. Everyone's got these postcode wars going on.
Where do you think that comes from? I think they think they're cool but they're not. They're not maturing. They're being very childish with the stuff they're doing as it's affecting other people's lives and even causing death.
What do you like about what this group is doing here? I like the fact that the mentors and the youth workers, they're easy to talk to. There's no pressure on talking to them. It's more fun and you don't feel judged. The kids here, we've all got something that connects us. Everyone here are really loving, really happy people. Very open people.
What do you think the difference is between you and kids getting into trouble? Self-respect. This has helped a lot. Getting people off the street. There's no problem here. Everyone's happy. Good vibe. It's better than just staying home.
What do you hope Mounty Yarns does in the future? I really hope they have bigger growth and more people. More people expressing how they feel and being themselves. No one needs to be judged.

transcripts.title: Withdrawn
transcripts.content:
[00:00:00]
Full name and spell it? My name's Polly, P-O-L-L-Y.
Tell us where you're from, how old you are. I am 13. I go to Mitchell High School. I live in Blacktown. I am in year eight.
Things you like? What do you like doing? I really like hanging out with people. I like caring for people. I love music as well. I love sport.
What's good and bad about the area? There are a lot of kids on the street that don't like school. A lot of them just hang around the street. That's a really bad thing for Mounty and Blacktown. But this is also a good way to get them off the street and start going back to school.
Why do you keep coming back to Mounty? I like this area a lot because I can express myself, I can be myself around these people. I don't have to hide my personality from people.
Tell us about what's happening today? We're building this whole back area into a more open space for outside activities.
What have you been doing? We have been painting. We're painting the aboriginal flag on the back of one of these containers.
Why is it important to have this space? Because it's a nice place to hang out with a lot of people. You can connect, express your ways of how you feel in what you're building and painting. You can connect with each other. You're socializing. Being able to talk and have fun and kick back.
What are your goals? Get a job. Start footy again. Start sport again.

[00:00:00]
Full name? Jaylee Watson, J-A-D-A-L-E-E W-A-T-S-O-N.
What is your hopes for Mounty? Personally Mounty is a pretty beautiful place. Hope to make it a better place. And also just a place for all the youth, younger kids. Just give 'em a place to come to and be interested in and help them out.
What are your hopes for the program? I'm hoping to influence other kids to join and do some stuff.
How long have you been on the program? For about nearly a year. I used to not come much at the start, like once every few weeks. But ever since now I just come like every week now, three, four times a week.
What are your goals? To better yourself and achieve more life skills.
What do you wanna do in the future? Kind of wanna help influence kids in that life to do stuff like this. Like a youth worker. But also want to do a bit of singing, making music.
What do you think about the community, the Aboriginal community? Pretty good. Pretty easy, pretty helpful.

[00:00:00]
Full name and spell it. Isaiah Benjamin Sines. I-S-A-I-A-H S-I-N-E-S from Mount Druitt here today. Just doing a project with the kids on the back of the yard here at Mount Druitt Aboriginal Community Service office.
What's this space and why do you want to fix this space up here? This space has been in the works for about three years now. A group of kids wanted a basketball court, a gym, and some other things. But today, finally kicking it off.
We've got two container ships with different cells from different countries. The first one is from Brisbane. The second one is Spain, and the last two is up for whatever the kids wanna make today.
Why is it important to have the kids involved here? This is for the kids. Without the input, we didn't know what they want. We can build anything here, but they're gonna use it and for them for the future. So it's good to have the kids involved.
Why this idea of bringing people here to engage with young people? Just turn up with open mind and just respectful of the kids and of the workplace. It's just a safe place for young people to come and enjoy themselves and just be kids again.
What's gonna be your favorite thing? The flag on the ground is gonna look amazing and I can't wait for the painting to be finished.
What are we building? The sprinkler system. The aboriginal flag on the ground. The walkway. The boys are finishing the framing off. The yarning circle and maybe eventually a fire pit.

transcripts.title: Withdrawn
transcripts.content:
[00:00:00]
Full name and spell it for me, your age. Archie Darcy, A-R-C-H-I-E. Darcy, D-A-R-C-Y. Tell us a tiny bit about yourself, brother. I'm a proud Wangkamarra and Camilla man. I'm from Burke and Warren. I lived everywhere. I class three places as my own town, so Burke, Warren and Mount Druitt. I've been back and forth my whole life.
Tell us a bit about what you do here at Mounty. I started here as a youth worker, and then I progressed into a case worker.
What's that involve? Basically it's just trying to break cycles for young kids. A lot of young kids deal with a lot of challenges in life. We're basically there to just remove the barriers that are stopping them from being a better version of themselves.
What sort of programs do you guys do with them? We do cooking programs, girls' gym program. We do cultural days and we do boxing programs. We try and mentor a proactive and healthy and positive lifestyle for young fellas.
What are the differences between what happens here and what happens within the system of corrections? The difference is we don't look at a piece of paper and go, oh, what a piece of paper says about a young person. All young people are good. Sometimes some kids are just in bad situations that are outta their control. We just see the young person as a young person, not as what the system makes 'em out to be.
What's going on today? We're building a safe space for young kids that we can bring into and teach 'em life skills, living skills. At the moment we've got the young kids helping out with the manual side of things.
Why is it important to pay kids for working with you guys? Unfortunately that's just a part of life. Basically reward 'em. When you're out there in the workforce, obviously you get paid for doing it.
What's your favorite part about what this place is gonna be? A safe space. Culturally it's like a meeting place. We wanna bring a culturally diverse background of any young person and any kind of person here and build a meeting place. Aboriginal people have been doing it for years. It's a togetherness kind of thing.

transcripts.title: Withdrawn
transcripts.content:
[00:00:00]
Full name? Spell it for me? T-A-J A-G-A C-A-M-P-B-E-L-L. Taj Aga Campbell. All right brother. Tell us a tiny bit about yourself. Um, 13, I'm from Liverpool, Windsor area. Yeah, just here today.
Tell us a bit about this area, about Mount Drew. It's pretty good. What's happening here today? We're just doing the backyard up. Why? To make it look better for the youth to come here, so they have a better spot.
And what do you do here normally? What programs do you do? Cooking, like gym programs.
What do you wanna do when you're older? I wanna be in the Army. Nice. What sort of army? Like engineer, infantry, everything. Any specific role? No, just any, any of 'em. I'd be happy.
And we're building a basketball court. Are you gonna be hooping on that? Yeah, hopefully. Who's your favorite player? I don't know. What are you looking forward to today? Just helping out and making it look better. Nice. And what's your favorite bit about this place? Probably being around the other youth.

Speaker 13: Can you introduce yourself?
Speaker 14: My name's Adam McKellar. I'm a Para Camilla and Wangkamarra man, my mob's from Burke mostly all over, but mostly from Burke.
Speaker 13: Why do you think it's important that young people speak up?
Speaker 14: Because young people are going through all of this stuff and why are the older fellas making choices for the younger fellas when they're the ones going through it?
Speaker 13: What are some ways that you, young people are already leading work in their own communities?
Speaker 14: Leading some policy change, grief and funerals, business projects.

transcripts.title: Withdrawn
transcripts.content:
[00:00:00]
Speaker 17: Can you introduce yourself?
Speaker 18: My name is Amelia Wyman. I'm a proud Mangpa Guara woman from Wilcannia.
Speaker 17: Why do you think it's important that young people speak up?
Speaker 18: I think it's important because normally young people don't get heard all the very much, like all the time dismissed. Their opinions aren't really appreciated, so it's very important that they get heard.
Speaker 17: What are some of the ways you or young people are already leading work in your own community?
Speaker 18: In my community, I'm one of the young leaders back at home. I teach dance with my dad's group. The kids, younger kids have a choir. Sometimes we have Desert P Media that comes out and does songs with the kids as well.
Speaker 17: How much control or power do you think young people have? Should they have more?
Speaker 18: Yes, young people definitely should have more power. They can point out the things that adults don't see. They can catch onto the small things that go unnoticed.
Speaker 17: What do you think adults think and assume about young people?
Speaker 18: I think that adults think about young children as naive, irresponsible, and stubborn. They would think that young people wouldn't know how to do anything 'cause they don't have experience. But I think the point is young people can do things if they are encouraged for it and they get nurtured towards that way and they get believed in, then they can also help make a difference.
Speaker 17: What made you interested in coming to today?
Speaker 18: It sparked an interest because it's some of the things that I can relate to and some of the problems that I wanna change as well. Here's a group of people that are talking about the same things that I want to talk about. So here's my chance to go and say something and try and help change it.
Speaker 17: What excites you about this work?
Speaker 18: Being able to make new friends, new connections, being able to make a difference in small communities, large communities within every single tribe or mob of aboriginal young people. Just coming together as one.

Speaker 28: Can you introduce yourself?
Speaker 29: Uh, my name's Taleigha Glover. I'm from Glebe. Um, yeah, my mob's Biripi from Kempsey.
Speaker 28: Why do you think it's important that young people speak up?
Speaker 29: Um, because the decisions that are being made aren't working. Yeah.
Speaker 28: What are some of the ways you or young people are already leading work in their own communities?
Speaker 29: Being involved in what we were doing today.
Speaker 28: How much control or power do you think young people have?
Speaker 29: Um, at the moment I think we are making moves to have more control in our lives and stuff.
Speaker 28: What do you think adults think and assume about young people?
Speaker 29: From experience, we're labeled. Troubled. Yeah.
Speaker 28: What made you interested in attending today?
Speaker 29: I wanted to be a part of just reinvest again yeah. And be a part of change.
Speaker 28: What excites you about this work?
Speaker 29: Being a part of change, motivating the next generation. Sharing our wisdom with the younger ones.

[00:00:00]
Speaker 30: Can you introduce yourself?
Speaker 31: I'm Leah. I am Hiri D and Camilla and I am from Mount Druitt.
Speaker 30: Why do you think it's important that young people speak up?
Speaker 31: Because if, I reckon young people should make decisions for themselves. 'cause if someone else makes a decision for them, it'll be the wrong one or they won't get it right. So I reckon young people should speak up. So they can tell their story, where they come from, what their problems are how we can help them, and yeah.
Speaker 30: What are some of the ways that you or young people are already leaving work in their own communities?
Speaker 31: We have the Youth Peak that me and Adam are running. It's just helping people young people who need help, who are in a bad position or just want someone to talk to in general.
Speaker 30: How much control or power do you think young people have?
Speaker 31: Compared to the government, not a lot, but the more young people that do speak up, I reckon we will get more power the more that young people speak up.
Speaker 30: What do you think adults think and assume about young people?
Speaker 31: Probably that we're immature, we're stupid. We do silly things which is like sometimes right? We do silly stuff sometimes, but everyone makes mistakes.
Speaker 30: What made you interested in attending today?
Speaker 31: It's like what we've been doing for the past year and a half since I've been working. It's like having a voice for other young people that I didn't have in foster care.
Speaker 30: What excites you about this work?
Speaker 31: Giving other young people a voice and deciding what they wanna do and making change.

transcripts.title: Withdrawn
transcripts.content:
[00:00:00]
Speaker 1: Can you introduce yourself?
Speaker 2: My name is Shayle McKellar. I'm a proud Wangkamarra man from Burke. I am a lived experience consultant and a youth worker. Primarily for Just Reinvest.
Speaker 1: Why do you think it's important that young people speak up?
Speaker 2: So, I was the kid that was locked up, you know, I was the kid that was dealt cards in the system. And I finished up getting my HSC in custody, I finished up, um, year 12, and then getting out and then working with all these young people, you know, there's some stuff that I went through in the system that was horrendous. And they wonder why these young people come out and not only have the trauma from, you know, they enter the system as victims, and then they come out as even more victimised people. So it's very important that you listen to these young people because they are the ones going through it, not the adults. And a lot of us have been through it and can have a very good understanding, but how we can actually combat these issues.
Speaker 1: What are some ways that you or young people are already leading work in their own communities?
Speaker 2: In the last 10 months of me being locked up, I finished up getting my HSC. And then out of that, I was given the opportunity to do my Cert 3, Cert 4 in Youth Work. And then I also became a lived experience consultant. So some of the things that I've been doing in this particular space with all these kids, like all these young people are kids that I've been working with for the last two to three years. And they're all from Mount Druitt.
Speaker 1: How much control or power do you think young people have?
Speaker 2: Like, they think they haven't got any, like when I was locked up, I thought I was gonna be nothing. But there's some pretty strong kids out there that are doing amazing things. And they just don't know it because they just think, yeah, they just think they're going about their daily business and they don't know how powerful they are.
Speaker 1: What do you think adults think and assume about young people?
Speaker 2: To be fair, I think a lot of these adults think these young people are just the problems, a lot of the time. But I feel like if you sat down and heard their stories, you would have a completely different outlook on a lot of these young people.
Speaker 1: What made you interested to attend today?
Speaker 2: To be really honest, we kinda put a lot of this together. And the reason why I love coming to this is because I just love working with young people. And we put this together to give the young people an opportunity to have their voice heard.
Speaker 1: How excited are you about this work?
Speaker 2: I'm really excited because it's not just about me, it's about a whole community of young people that are going to be able to have their voices heard and make real change.

transcripts.title: Withdrawn
transcripts.content:
[00:00:00]
Speaker 3: Can you introduce yourself?
Speaker 4: Yeah, my name's Tyrese, originally from work, but I've been in Dubbo and recently got involved with just reinvest. So here I am today.
Speaker 3: What why do you think it's important that young people speak up?
Speaker 4: It's pretty good for young people speak up because sometimes they might have something might be about them, but it'd be like other people. Like making decision for 'em instead of them making decisions for themselves. So I think it is, yeah, it's very good for the young boys to speak up for themselves. Yeah.
Speaker 3: What are some ways that you, young people are already leading work in their own communities?
Speaker 4: I'll probably just say maybe we're given a chance to the younger generation, like a voice that speaks, maybe something like us and another generation never had, yeah, I think it's pretty good just giving the young boys a voice.
Speaker 3: How much control or power do you think young people have?
Speaker 4: Like for the situation that the boys locked up in that situation, they probably wouldn't have a lot of power. Probably, maybe none. 'cause everyone's making decisions for 'em, like telling them what to do and like how to do it. So yeah, probably not a lot.
Speaker 3: What do you think adults think and assume about young people?
Speaker 4: Probably like you, you said my brother just based on what they see, like of that young fella, like they see him doing bad. Like they probably got nothing good to say about him. They see him doing good, they'd be like, skying him, mum. So yeah, that's probably main thing based on their actions.
Speaker 3: What made you interest to attend today?
Speaker 4: I don't know, bro. I just, yeah. I'm just happy to be a part of something bigger, help them. Yeah. Just maybe put something there for the boys that maybe we never had, yeah. So like it's helping like giving back to the community.
